title: When the System Starts to Drift
pressure: Weeks after defining the integrity system, operations have normalized. However, subtle variations appear across offices, and no one is certain whether the system is still being applied consistently.
tradeoff: Assume the system holds through trust, or actively verify and enforce it under real operational drift.

incident.roleShift: from system chooser to system enforcer under drift

propagation.exitEffect: The system slowly becomes optional and dependent on individual interpretation.
propagation.entryEffect: The system becomes resilient because it is tested and reinforced under variation.
propagation.reExposureNote: Drift reveals whether the system exists without direct oversight.

action_contract.description: Detect and respond to early system drift with a concrete, observable enforcement mechanism.

AIR_logic.success: System consistency is maintained across offices regardless of local pressure.
AIR_logic.miss: System becomes interpretive and varies depending on leadership presence.

forced_reset.trigger: A staff member reports that the integrity system is being applied differently in another office.
forced_reset.action: Compare cases, identify deviation, and enforce a unified standard.

pattern_detection.passive_trust: Assumes system is working without verification.
pattern_detection.localized_system: Allows each office to reinterpret the system.
pattern_detection.relationship_buffer: Avoids accountability when relationships are involved.
pattern_detection.drift_normalization: Treats inconsistency as natural variation instead of system failure.

A.escalation_text: A second report comes in showing a different office handled a similar integrity issue with a private exception.
A.pressure_increase: The system is now diverging without visibility.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to confirm whether the system is actually consistent?
action_decision.context: Multiple offices may already be operating under different interpretations.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, request and compare two recent integrity decisions from different offices using the same criteria.
Remind leaders to stay aligned with the system moving forward.

second_choices.label: Assume it was a one-time judgment call
Request documentation of how the decision was made
second_choices.cost: Introduces scrutiny that may be resisted.

B.escalation_text: Offices begin justifying different versions of the system, each claiming their situation is unique.
B.pressure_increase: The system is fragmenting into multiple definitions.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to prevent system fragmentation?
action_decision.context: Without boundaries, each office becomes its own system.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, publish 3 non-negotiable system rules that apply across all offices.
Encourage offices to use judgment based on their situation.

second_choices.label: Accept variation as necessary for flexibility
second_choices.cost: Destroys the concept of a unified system.

second_choices.label: Define non-negotiable elements of the system

C.escalation_text: A trusted leader bypasses the system quietly, and staff notice but do not escalate.
C.pressure_increase: Silence becomes a signal that accountability is selective.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to show accountability is not relationship-dependent?
action_decision.context: The system is tested most when influential people are involved.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, document and apply the same follow-up process to the trusted leader case with visible tracking.
Address the issue privately and move forward.

second_choices.label: Handle it privately to preserve trust
Apply visible accountability regardless of relationship

D.escalation_text: Your checks reveal inconsistencies across multiple offices, and leaders begin to feel monitored.

action_decision.prompt: What will you do within 72 hours to ensure the system holds without your constant presence?
action_decision.context: A system is real only if it survives without direct oversight.

choices.label: Within 72 hours, implement a monthly cross-office integrity audit comparing decisions and outcomes.

second_choices.label: Install a recurring drift detection mechanism

ending_identity_choice.prompt: When no one is watching closely, what kind of system are you running?

choices.label: A system that depends on trust and varies quietly
A system that detects and corrects its own drift
Trust that the system is being followed without direct checks
choices.subtext: It feels efficient because constant verification seems unnecessary if leaders are aligned.

choices.label: Allow each office to adapt the system to their situation
choices.subtext: It feels respectful of operational reality since each office faces different pressures.

choices.label: Avoid direct enforcement where strong relationships are involved
choices.subtext: It feels stabilizing because pushing too hard could damage trust with key people.

choices.label: Actively test whether the system holds under variation
choices.subtext: It creates friction because it may expose inconsistency and require correction.
